Kidneys process about 180 litres of blood and remove almost 2 litres of waste every day. Kidney diseases and injuries can affect how kidneys function. Over a period of time, untreated kidney diseases can result in a slow and silent decline in kidney function. Some of the diseases, such as diabetic kidney disease, blood pressure, glomerular diseases, congenital kidney diseases, trauma, or poisoning, can cause damage to the nephrons, the filtering units of kidneys, and thus reduce the kidney function or renal function.

Renal function or kidney function is intact when both the kidneys are working efficiently, as measured by the estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR). eGFR is the measure of the percentage of kidney function that is available. Rarely, a small or mild decline (30 to 40%) is noticed even in normal individuals. People with reduced kidney function usually have an associated kidney disease , and there are chances of both of them worsening with age. Kidney diseases and decline in kidney function are usually silent, until the decline is severe with an eGFR of 25%. Any further decline below 10–15% requires lifetime dialysis or renal transplant.

Our kidney transplant centres are situated at 3 locations, offering a wide range of treatment options for kidney diseases with a success rate of 90%. Our kidney transplant program is aimed at providing quality services for patients with end-stage kidney disease and chronic kidney disease.

Over the years, Yashoda Institute of Kidney Transplantation has successfully conducted 1,000+ transplants in patients from Telangana, Andhra Pradesh, and nearby states. Our kidney transplant institutes are situated at 3 locations, offering a wide range of treatment options for kidney diseases with a success rate of 90%, making it the best hospital for kidney transplant in Hyderabad .

For patients suffering from chronic kidney disease (CKD) or end-stage renal disease (ESRD), a kidney transplant remains as the best treatment option compared to dialysis. A kidney transplant provides a better quality of life and longer survival in such patients. Normally kidney transplant is done after matching the blood groups of the donor and the recipient and after checking the compatibility in order to avoid rejection and auto immune responses. When the recipient and donor have matching blood groups during a kidney transplant, it is called an ABO kidney transplant., which is the standard and preferred type of transplant because it reduces the risk of the recipient’s immune system rejecting the new kidney. When such a match is not possible, ABO-incompatible kidney transplantation is performed, involving pre- and post-transplant medical treatments to lower the body’s antibodies and prevent rejection.
Health Blogs for Kidney

Unmatched expertise, state-of-the-art facilities, and a dedicated team with a proven record of success in providing personalised care, from pre-surgery preparation to post-operative recovery, make us the best choice for patients looking for a kidney transplant.
Diseases & Treatments

faqs.q: How is an ABO incompatible transplant done?
faqs.a: ABO incompatible transplant can be successfully performed by removing antibodies from the donor’s blood before transplantation and reducing the risk of rejecting the donor kidney. This process is known as desensitization and allows for a wider pool of donors to be considered for kidney transplants, ultimately increasing the chances of finding a suitable match for the recipient.

faqs.q: What is a kidney transplant?
faqs.a: Kidney transplant is a surgical procedure that involves the removal of an effected kidney and replacing it with a healthy kidney. The kidney can be transplanted from a dead donor or a living donor.

faqs.q: How is a kidney transplant done?
faqs.a: The procedure includes selecting a suitable donor, carrying out medical examinations, executing kidney replacement surgery, and providing continuous immunosuppressive medicine post-transplant surgery.

faqs.q: What are the benefits of kidney transplantation over dialysis?
faqs.a: Kidney transplantation allows patients to have a more normal life without frequent dialysis or dietary restrictions and improves quality of life by facilitating flexibility in daily activities,work.

faqs.q: What’s the difference between living and deceased donor kidney transplants?
faqs.a: Living donor kidney transplant surgery involves shorter waiting periods and a higher success rate. Transplants using deceased donors provide possibilities for recipients without living donors, although they may have lower success rates because they employ kidneys from recently deceased patients.

faqs.q: What tests are done before a kidney transplant?
faqs.a: Tests include blood and urine tests, imaging scans, cardiac evaluations, biopsies, and crossmatch tests to ensure compatibility and overall health.

faqs.q: Why is immunosuppressive medication needed after a kidney transplant?
faqs.a: These medications prevent the body from rejecting the new kidney by suppressing the immune system, and they must be taken lifelong with regular monitoring.

faqs.q: How long can a person live with a kidney transplant?
faqs.a: A successful transplant involves various factors. A kidney transplant from a living donor can last 12–25 years, while a kidney from a deceased donor can last 8–20 years.

pages.title: Best Liver Transplant Hospital in Hyderabad/India
overview: The liver is remarkable for its unique regenerative ability, capable of fully regrowing even after up to 70% of its mass has been removed. This rapid regrowth allows the liver to restore its full function within weeks, distinguishing it from other human organs. Since there is no viable medical or mechanical alternative to fully replicate liver function, severe and irreversible liver failure may necessitate a liver transplant as a life-saving intervention.

Liver transplantation/ Hepatic Transplant Surgery is a procedure that involves replacing the diseased liver with a healthy donor liver (commonly referred to as liver replacement surgery). This surgery has been performed for over 4 decades, providing thousands of people worldwide the opportunity to lead normal lives. Liver transplant surgery has proven beneficial in both adults and children.

Liver transplantation is often a life-saving procedure for individuals with end-stage liver disease. Traditionally, liver transplants required compatibility between the blood types of the donor and recipient, limiting the availability of suitable organs. However, with advancements in medical science, ABO-incompatible (ABOi) liver transplantation offers a promising alternative and new hope to critically ill patients who may not have a compatible donor available.

In ABO-incompatible liver transplantation, the donor and recipient have different blood types. This approach expands the pool of potential donors for patients in need of a liver transplant, as it allows for transplantation across blood type barriers. Unlike ABO-compatible transplants, where the donor and recipient have compatible blood types, ABO incompatible transplants require additional strategies to prevent immune rejection of the transplanted liver. Various strategies, like desensitization, have been introduced to overcome the complications of ABO incompatibility.

With advancements in transplant science and medical therapy, it has been seen that the success rates of ABO-Incompatible liver transplantation have been reported to be similar to that of ABO compatible living donor liver transplantation (ABO-C LDLT). Approaching the right hospital with fully operative advanced infrastructure alongside an experienced panel of liver transplant surgeons is the key to the success rates of a liver transplant.
The success of ABO incompatible liver transplantation hinges on several key factors:

Liver transplantation is often essential for treating chronic liver disease or acute liver failure. In adults, the primary cause of liver transplants is liver cirrhosis, a condition where healthy liver cells are replaced with scar tissue due to various liver injuries. Cirrhosis can result from viral infections like hepatitis B and C, alcohol consumption, autoimmune liver diseases, fat accumulation in the liver, and hereditary liver diseases.

In children, the most common reason for liver transplantation is biliary atresia, a condition characterised by damaged or missing bile ducts that block bile flow and lead to cirrhosis. Other conditions that may require liver transplantation include certain liver cancers, benign liver tumours, and hereditary diseases. Sudden or rapidly developing liver failure can occur in both children and adults, often due to viral infections or adverse reactions to medications, including painkiller overdoses or the use of certain herbal and traditional medicines.

The Department of Hepatology & Liver Transplant at Yashoda Hospitals offers a comprehensive, multidisciplinary approach to liver disease management, adhering to international standards and guidelines. Our services encompass liver transplant surgery, liver resection surgeries (both open and laparoscopic), liver critical care, liver pathology, and liver radiology committed to offering the highest quality care for a wide range of liver ailments and liver-related diseases. Our team of specialists strives to provide the best liver transplant treatment personalised to specific patient needs.

They are also proficient in living donor liver transplants, including dual-lobe, domino, swap, ABO-incompatible liver transplantation, combined liver-kidney transplantation, and liver transplant from deceased donors.

faqs.q: Can the liver be transplanted?
faqs.a: The liver is a remarkable organ with a unique capacity to regenerate itself. It can be transplanted as a whole organ from a deceased donor, or split or partial liver tissue can be transplanted from a living donor.

faqs.q: What is a liver transplant?
faqs.a: A liver transplant involves replacing a diseased or failing liver with a healthy one from either a deceased or living donor. This procedure is used to treat end-stage liver disease, liver failure, or specific types of liver cancer, aiming to restore liver function and enhance the recipient’s overall well-being.

faqs.q: How does a liver transplant work?
faqs.a: In a liver transplant procedure, the patient’s diseased liver is surgically removed and replaced with a healthy liver obtained from either a deceased or living donor, often a family member or close relative.

faqs.q: When is a liver transplant needed?
faqs.a: A liver transplant is typically needed when a person’s liver is severely damaged or fails completely due to conditions such as cirrhosis, liver cancer, acute liver failure, or genetic disorders affecting liver function.

faqs.q: Is liver transplant safe for donors?
faqs.a: Liver donation, whether from a living or deceased donor, is generally considered safe. A thorough medical evaluation is mandatory to avoid some common potential risks.

faqs.q: Is a liver transplant successful?
faqs.a: Yes, liver transplant surgery is often successful in restoring liver function and improving the recipient’s quality of life, though it carries risks and requires careful postoperative management.

faqs.q: Can liver cancer be cured with a liver transplant?
faqs.a: Liver transplants can potentially cure liver cancer in select cases. However, it depends on several factors, such as the size and number of tumours, the absence of spread beyond the liver, and meeting specific criteria for transplantation.

pages.title: Lung Transplant Hospital in Hyderabad
overview: Lung transplant is a surgical procedure to replace diseased or failing lung with a healthy lung, usually from donor depending on the medical condition. It may involve replacing one or both of the lungs. Diseases which can damage your lungs are;

The disease process starts with decreasing oxygen levels in the blood and as it progresses. Oxygen will have to be given continuously to the patient. At this stage usually lung transplant is done. The matching of the person with the donor from whom lung is to be taken is done on basis of: blood type, size of organ (by comparing the rate and chest measurement) and also matching of the tissue typing elements. The patient will be investigated and put on waiting list of the “Jeevandan” organ donation program. When a suitable donor is available, a team prepares the patient for the transplant surgery. Another team will be sent for procuring the organ. The patient’s chest will be opened and the diseased lung will be removed. The airway and the blood vessels will be connected to the donor lung. After the surgery patient will have to spend a few days in the ICU, and the bronchoscopy will be done as and when required. After discharge from the hospital, there will be three months of frequent monitoring by the lung transplantation team . The patient is prescribed immunosuppressant medications, which need to be taken life-long.

faqs.q: Why Yashoda Mother and Child Institute?
faqs.a: Special care at every step of your journey to motherhood

faqs.q: Why is it performed?
faqs.a: A 2D Echocardiogram, or 2D Echo test, uses ultrasound waves to create moving images of the heart’s internal structures. This non-invasive diagnostic tool helps assess the heart’s function and identify issues like clots, blockages, and congenital heart defects.

faqs.q: Why is it performed?
faqs.a: A 4D scan uses ultrasound to capture real-time, 3d images of a baby’s movements and growth in the womb. It confirms gestational age, due date, checks for abnormalities or birth defects, and shows the baby’s body and facial expressions. This non-invasive, risk-free procedure helps confirm multiple pregnancies and should be discussed with a doctor.

faqs.q: Why is it performed?
faqs.a: An AFP (alpha-fetoprotein) test measures the levels of this protein in a blood sample, which in healthy males and non-pregnant females could indicate cancer. In pregnant women, the test is used to detect potential chromosomal or developmental abnormalities in the fetus, with levels that are either too high or too low indicating a potential problem.

faqs.q: Why is it performed?
faqs.a: Antenatal Care (ANC) lab tests are performed during the first trimester of pregnancy to thoroughly assess the health of the mother and her baby. These tests are crucial for ensuring a safe pregnancy and can screen for infectious diseases like HIV, syphilis, and Hepatitis B that could be transmitted to the child. Therefore, they are an essential tool for providing the utmost care and precaution during this critical phase of pregnancy.

faqs.q: Why is it performed?
faqs.a: The Beta hCG test measures hormone levels of Human Chorionic Gonadotropin (hCG) in blood, produced by the placenta after fertilisation. Used to confirm pregnancy, estimate fetal age, diagnose miscarriage, or screen for Down syndrome. It also helps evaluate some cancers and conditions. Levels are low early in pregnancy, then rise rapidly, peaking by the end of the first trimester.

faqs.q: Why is it performed?
faqs.a: A double marker test is a diagnostic procedure that uses a blood sample and an ultrasound to screen for chromosomal abnormalities in a fetus. Performed during the first trimester, this test is used to detect potential neurological problems, like Down syndrome and Edward’s syndrome.

faqs.q: Why is it performed?
faqs.a: An NT (nuchal translucency) scan is a safe, non-invasive ultrasound performed during the first trimester of pregnancy to assess the risk of chromosomal abnormalities. The test measures the thickness of the fluid at the back of the baby’s neck, which may indicate conditions like Down’s syndrome, Edward’s syndrome, or a congenital cardiac disorder. While it has a 70% accuracy rate, the NT scan is a screening tool, not a definitive diagnostic test, and only indicates whether the baby is at a high or low risk.

Health Blogs for Movement Disorder
Doctor Talk
Health Talk

Movement disorders are primarily caused by neurological conditions like stroke, Parkinson’s disease, and head injuries, as well as other conditions like infections,and genetic issues. Movement disorders can also result from certain medications and exposure to toxic substances.

Although there are many causes of movement disorders, genetics is one of them. Examples of hereditary movement disorders include a few conditions like Huntington’s disease, Wilson’s disease, and others.

Indeed, the underlying causes of movement disorders determine the likelihood of recovery. Usually, if a person’s parkinsonian symptoms are caused by a medication, the symptoms can be alleviated by quitting the drug. While chronic cases cannot be cured, they can be effectively managed with treatments such as deep brain stimulation etc to improve quality of life .
Diseases & Treatments

faqs.q: What causes movement disorders?
faqs.a: Movement disorders are primarily caused by neurological conditions like stroke, Parkinson’s disease, and head injuries, as well as other conditions like infections,and genetic issues. Movement disorders can also result from certain medications and exposure to toxic substances.

faqs.q: What are the common symptoms of movement disorders?
faqs.a: The individual with movement disorder experiences certain symptoms, such as tremors, jerky movements, difficulty in walking, swallowing, writing, talking, along with stiffness in limbs and trouble with balance and coordination.

faqs.q: How are movement disorders diagnosed?
faqs.a: Certain imaging tests, like CT and MRI, as well as specific blood tests and electrical activity assessment tests, like ECG and EEG, are carried out to assess the state of the brain, spinal cord, or nerves and determine the cause of movement disorders.

faqs.q: Are movement disorders hereditary?
faqs.a: Although there are many causes of movement disorders, genetics is one of them. Examples of hereditary movement disorders include a few conditions like Huntington’s disease, Wilson’s disease, and others.

faqs.q: Can movement disorders be cured?
faqs.a: Indeed, the underlying causes of movement disorders determine the likelihood of recovery. Usually, if a person’s parkinsonian symptoms are caused by a medication, the symptoms can be alleviated by quitting the drug. While chronic cases cannot be cured, they can be effectively managed with treatments such as deep brain stimulation etc to improve quality of life .

faqs.q: How do movement disorders progress over time?
faqs.a: If left untreated, movement disorders worsen over time, affecting both sides of the body, making it increasingly difficult to walk, talk, and carry out daily tasks.

faqs.q: How does stress affect movement disorders?
faqs.a: Due to its effects on the nervous system, stress is one of the factors that can exacerbate symptoms like stiffness and tremors. Additionally, it disrupts movement and coordination.

faqs.q: Can children develop movement disorders?
faqs.a: Yes, children can also have movement disorders as a result of brain damage, head injuries, or genetic causes; therefore, early diagnosis and treatment are necessary.

pages.title: Multiorgan Transplant
overview: Organ transplantation, a complex yet beneficial procedure, is employed in individuals with end-stage organ failure, & failure of more than one organ in the body is termed “multiple organ failure”. This is often caused by certain underlying conditions, such as amyloidosis, which is characterized by deposition of abnormal proteins in the kidney, liver, & heart, thereby causing severe damage. Initially, to restore this damage & the functioning of a particular organ or organs affected, treatments such as IV fluids, oxygenation therapy, along with mechanical support are provided to improve the symptoms. However, if these treatments fail to slow the progression, a multi-organ transplant is a possible option for improving quality of life by replacing damaged organs with healthy ones, giving such individuals a second chance at life.

Organ donors might be alive or deceased. The majority of deceased contributions come from people who have been declared brain dead, a state characterized by irreversible loss of brain function, which is frequently caused by life-threatening injury, stroke, or a lack of oxygen to the brain.

Yashoda Hospitals’ multi-organ transplant department ensures proficiency and in-depth expertise in all aspects of care, from patient selection to organ retrieval, the transplant itself, pre- and post-inpatient care, equipment, infrastructure, and a multidisciplinary team of specialists to achieve the best results. Transplant patients receive personalized physiotherapy, diet, rehabilitation, and psychology services to ensure the best possible outcomes.

Health Blogs for Multiorgan Transplant

Certain medical conditions can impact multiple organs, resulting in organ failure in more than one system. In some cases, different illnesses or treatments can lead to unrelated organ failures. A common cause of multiple organ failure is a multisystem disease known as amyloidosis.
Diseases & Treatments

faqs.q: Faq’s What is a multiple organ transplant called?
faqs.a: A multiple organ transplant, also called a dual or multi-organ transplant, is a complex surgery where two or more failing organs are replaced with healthy ones from a donor. This procedure aims to restore normal function and significantly enhance the patient’s quality of life.

faqs.q: What is the most common multiorgan transplant?
faqs.a: The most common multi-organ transplants include heart-kidney, heart-liver, kidney-pancreas, and heart-lung. Though less common than single-organ transplants, the field is expanding. Multi-organ transplants require specialized care and a coordinated team to manage the complex process, with approaches varying based on the type of transplant.

faqs.q: Who is eligible for a multiorgan transplant?
faqs.a: Certain medical conditions can impact multiple organs, resulting in organ failure in more than one system. In some cases, different illnesses or treatments can lead to unrelated organ failures. A common cause of multiple organ failure is a multisystem disease known as amyloidosis.

faqs.q: What is the most successful organ transplant?
faqs.a: Heart and kidney transplants are among the most successful procedures in transplantation.

faqs.q: How are organ donors matched for multiorgan transplants?
faqs.a: Organ donors are matched to recipients based on factors like blood type, organ size, geography, medical urgency, and waiting time. Blood type compatibility is crucial, with type O as the universal donor and type AB as the universal recipient. Organ size matters, especially for children, and proximity to the donor hospital is considered. Medical urgency and the recipient’s time on the waiting list also influence the match.

faqs.q: How do I get on the waiting list for organ transplants?
faqs.a: To get on the organ transplant waiting list in India, you can be referred by your doctor, contact a transplant hospital, schedule an evaluation, and get listed at multiple hospitals. The process includes health evaluations, understanding the risks and benefits, signing a consent form, and getting vaccinated to reduce post-transplant infection risk. When an organ becomes available, recipients are matched based on medical urgency, compatibility, time on the waiting list, and proximity to the donor.

pages.title: Best Kidney Hospital in Hyderabad, India
overview: Kidneys process about 180 litres of blood and remove almost 2 litres of waste daily. Renal diseases and injuries may impair renal functionality. Over time, the absence of treatment for kidney diseases can lead to a gradual and insidious deterioration of renal function. Certain conditions, including diabetic nephropathy, hypertension, glomerular diseases, congenital kidney disorders, trauma, or poisoning, can inflict damage upon the nephrons, the filtering units of the kidneys, consequently diminishing renal functionality.

Nephrology is a specialised branch of medicine focused on the study, diagnosis, and treatment of a variety of kidney diseases and conditions. Nephrologists specialise in treating several structural, functional, and morphological diseases of the kidneys and their internal structures. The nephrology department at Yashoda Hospitals is dedicated to providing high-quality services with advanced facilities and personalised patient care. Our nephrologists offer a full spectrum of diagnostic, consultative, and treatment services for kidney disease patients, from the earliest detectable changes in kidney function to end-stage kidney disorders.

Diseases and Conditions affecting the renal system frequently manifest as chronic kidney failure, a condition observed in chronic renal diseases that leads to gradual, irreversible damage to the kidneys and a decline in renal function. In the early stages, the signs are often subtle. Therefore, early diagnosis and intervention for renal diseases are of paramount importance.

Acute kidney injury (AKI) is commonly seen in hospitalised patients, especially in critically ill patients. The institute provides critical care with leading practising nephrologists for mild, moderate, and severe acute kidney injury. AKI is associated with a rapid (within hours or days) decrease in renal excretion and accumulation of creatinine and toxic wastes (accelerated decline in kidney function). Our nephrologists focus on identifying the injury before it culminates in acute renal failure. Patients may require renal replacement therapy in both cases. It is important to stop the progression of acute renal failure to chronic kidney disease.

Advanced Robotic, Laparoscopic, and Laser-Assisted Surgeries

Kidneys process about 180 litres of blood and remove almost 2 litres of waste daily. Renal diseases and injuries may impair renal functionality. Over time, the absence of treatment for kidney diseases can lead to a gradual and insidious deterioration of renal function. Certain conditions, including diabetic nephropathy, hypertension, glomerular diseases, congenital kidney disorders, trauma, or poisoning, can inflict damage upon the nephrons, the filtering units of the kidneys, consequently diminishing renal functionality.

Nephrology is a specialised branch of medicine focused on the study, diagnosis, and treatment of a variety of kidney diseases and conditions. Nephrologists specialise in treating several structural, functional, and morphological diseases of the kidneys and their internal structures. The nephrology department at Yashoda Hospitals is dedicated to providing high-quality services with advanced facilities and personalised patient care. Our nephrologists offer a full spectrum of diagnostic, consultative, and treatment services for kidney disease patients, from the earliest detectable changes in kidney function to end-stage kidney disorders.

In the early stages, the signs are often subtle. Therefore, early diagnosis and intervention for renal diseases are of paramount importance.

faqs.q: Why is it performed?
faqs.a: Kidney function markers are crucial to determining the glomerular filtration rate (GFR), which measures how well the kidneys filter out waste products from the blood, such as creatinine, urea, and albumin.

faqs.q: Why is it performed?
faqs.a: The electrolyte panel is a blood test conducted to evaluate kidney function by monitoring the concentrations of electrolytes, including sodium , potassium, calcium, and magnesium. This test also facilitates diagnosis and monitoring of conditions, such as chronic kidney disease and acute kidney injury, while managing fluid and electrolyte imbalances.

faqs.q: Why is it performed?
faqs.a: To evaluate the extent of inflammation associated with chronic kidney disease, two notable inflammatory markers must be considered, namely C-reactive protein (CRP) and Interleukin-6 (IL-6). These markers assess the progression of chronic kidney disease and the concomitant increased risk of cardiovascular disease, respectively.

faqs.q: Why is it performed?
faqs.a: Autoimmune markers help identify autoantibodies, antibodies that target the kidneys, like anti-glomerular basement membrane antibodies found in Goodpasture syndrome, that cause kidney damage. It also identifies the underlying mechanism of autoimmune kidney disease.

faqs.q: Why is it performed?
faqs.a: The uric acid test measures the uric acid levels in blood or urine. Its primary aim is to diagnose and monitor gout, kidney stones, and other metabolic disorders to assess kidney function. It is performed against conditions like hyperuricemia (high uric acid levels), adding to nephrolithiasis (kidney stones) and worsening chronic kidney disease (CKD).

faqs.q: Why is it performed?
faqs.a: Urinalysis, commonly referred to as a urine routine test, is a general body test to detect the signs of infections, kidney infections, or diabetes from a urine sample. It checks for physical, chemical, and microscopic properties and detects conditions like urinary tract infections, kidney diseases, diabetes, and liver problems.

faqs.q: Why is it performed?
faqs.a: This test aims to identify and determine the sensitivity of bacteria or microorganisms in the urine and is particularly performed to diagnose urinary tract infections. It identifies the bacteria or fungi causing the infections and determines which antibiotics are useful against the condition.

faqs.q: Why is it performed?

faqs.q: Why is it performed?

faqs.q: Why is it performed?

pages.title: Nuclear Medicine Doctors in Hyderabad
overview: Nuclear medicine is a specialty that involves non-invasive, painless, safe and cost-effective modalities of medical diagnosis and treatment. Nuclear and molecular medicine imaging and therapies have paved paths to offer best medical care for serious conditions such as benign tumors and cancers, thyroid and other endocrine abnormalities, and neurodegenerative diseases.
Modern-day diagnostic procedures explore the utility of radiation and radio-pharmaceuticals to take diagnosis one step forward.

In nuclear medicine, radioactive tracers are used in small quantities (administered to the patient through IV or oral route or inhalation) to visualize the real-time function of a body. These radiopharmaceuticals, when administered to the patient, can be recorded using special cameras. The resulting images provide accurate detailing and contrast of the affected areas in the body. Nuclear medicine and molecular imaging help to create high-quality images of the abnormalities, which are either deep-rooted or camouflaged. Thus, nuclear medicine imaging obviates the need for expensive diagnostic procedures and invasive surgeries.

Nuclear medicine imaging (or radionuclide imaging) identifies the functional abnormalities such as areas of varying activity in the brain, blood flow to the respective organ, pumping efficiency of the heart, areas of bone healing, cancer progress.

The images can point out the defects at the molecular level, which are the earliest signs of diseases. Thus, nuclear medicine imaging becomes an essential part of cancer diagnosis as it helps in the staging of cancer and earliest detection of metastases (the spread of cancer).

Nuclear medicine physicians also use radionucleotides (radiopharmaceuticals) to treat cancer and other medical conditions such as thyroid diseases. Radio-pharmaceuticals are available as oral formulations, injectables and inhalants. These radiopharmaceuticals are different from those used for nuclear medicine imaging (gamma emitters), in a way that offers better biological utility and short penetration into the tissue (alpha or beta emitters).

Treatments:

Nuclear medicine uses radioactive materials to diagnose and treat various conditions by evaluating organ function, detecting cancers like thyroid disease and neuroendocrine tumors, relieving pain (e.g., arthritic pain), and addressing issues in the brain, bones, heart, kidneys, lungs, and thyroid. It also helps assess blood flow and function in organs like the heart.
Diseases are treated by nuclear medicine?

Nuclear medicine offers effective treatments for various conditions, including thyroid diseases, where radioactive iodine therapy is used for hyperthyroidism and thyroid cancer. It also alleviates metastatic bone pain and arthritic discomfort. Additionally, it addresses neuroendocrine tumors—rare growths found in the pancreas), small bowel, or rectum—through advanced therapeutic techniques.

faqs.q: Why is it performed?
faqs.a: Detailed images of the tumor and surrounding tissues aid in cancer staging, treatment planning, and monitoring treatment response. Its purpose is to improve the diagnosis and treatment decision.

faqs.q: Why is it performed?
faqs.a: HD PET scanning transforms PET/CT imaging by providing consistent, high-resolution, distortion-free images that enable even 2mm lesions, significantly improving diagnosis and treatment monitoring.

faqs.q: Why is it performed?
faqs.a: The e-cam signature gamma camera enhances SPECT imaging by leveraging advanced technology to deliver superior image quality, quick patient throughput, and greater comfort.

faqs.q: Why is it performed?
faqs.a: Digital mammography aims to provide a clearer and more accurate image of breast tissue for early breast cancer detection and other conditions. Its purpose is to improve the efficiency of breast cancer screening, diagnosis and monitoring.

faqs.q: Why is it performed?
faqs.a: In the field of radiation oncology, the Varian RapidArc is a specialised linear accelerator that is employed to administer precise, intensely modulated radiation therapy. Its primary objective is to enhance the effectiveness of cancer treatment by precisely directing radiation toward tumours, minimising the impact on healthy tissues in the vicinity, and documenting the results within a single rotation of the treatment machine.

faqs.q: What causes cancer, and how many stages are there?
faqs.a: The primary reason for the patient to develop cancer is the genetic mutation of DNA in cells, leading to uncontrolled spread. These changes may result from environmental factors like long-term exposure to radiation and carcinogens, or they may be hereditary. There are four stages of cancer, where the patient typically possesses more advanced and widespread disease as the stages of cancer progress.

faqs.q: What are the symptoms of cancer?
faqs.a: The symptoms of cancer vary a lot among individuals and depend on the type and stage of the cancer. The general symptoms of cancer include fatigue, lumps or swelling, persistent cough, unusual bleeding, changes in bowel or bladder habits, and weight loss for no valid reason.

faqs.q: What tests can diagnose cancer?
faqs.a: Various blood tests are conducted to check for tumour markers, while imaging tests such as ultrasound, CT, MRI, and PET scans provide detailed images of soft tissues and highlight areas of high metabolic activity. Biopsy procedures involve analysing cells under a microscope to determine the nature of a suspicious lump or growth. Mammography is specifically used to diagnose breast cancer, and Pap tests, along with HPV testing, are used to screen for and detect cervical cancer.

pages.title: What is Pancreas Transplantation?
overview: Pancreatic transplantation is a major surgical procedure in which a healthy pancreas, usually from a brain-dead donor, is implanted into a patient whose pancreas fails to produce insulin. This is usually a type I diabetic patient (as well as some type 2 diabetics) whose pancreas fails to produce the required insulin. This is a specialized procedure and is performed by a pancreas transplant surgeon.

Once the pancreas is removed from the donor, it is kept viable by cooling it and preserving it in an ice-cold preservative solution. It can be preserved only for a few hours after it is removed from the donor. In addition, the donor’s blood type must be compatible with the patient receiving the organ so that the body has a better chance of accepting the organ.

For people with insulin-dependent diabetes who develop renal failure and are in need of kidney transplantation, transplantation of both pancreas and kidney is a viable option for better life and longevity. Some with insulin-dependent diabetes but with preserved kidney function can have other life-threatening complications and a poor quality of life. For them, pancreas transplantation alone is a reasonable option.

overview: Yashoda Hospitals proudly hosts a dedicated team of specialists who are experienced in treating movement disorders like Parkinson’s Disease with personalised care and advanced treatment modalities. The team has an exclusive experience of successfully treating patients with parkison’s disease by improving their motor function with the most effective medical and surgical interventions and also healing them holistically by using various supportive therapies like speech therapy, occupational therapy ,lifestyle modifications and psychological counselling for the involved families as well.

Parkinson’s Disease is a chronic and progressive neurodegenerative disease. which is characterised by gradual degeneration of dopamine-producing neurons in a specific region of the brain called the substantia nigra. Dopamine is a neurotransmitter that plays a crucial role in coordinating smooth and controlled muscle movements. When dopamine levels decrease, communication between nerve cells becomes impaired, leading to the hallmark motor symptoms of PD.

At Yashoda Hospitals, our expert team provides the best treatment for parkinson’s disease in Hyderabad,India providing comprehensive care for patients at every stage of their journey. Our neurology and neurosurgery teams collaborate to perform detailed brain imaging and cognitive evaluations, ensuring the best possible treatment outcomes. Our treatment plans are personalised to each patient’s needs, from early-stage management with medicines to non-pharmacological therapies such as physical, occupational, and speech therapy. faqs.q: Why Choose Yashoda for Pediatric Endocrinology?
faqs.a: 30+ Years of Unshakable Trust: We bring decades of institutional experience to every pediatric consultation.

faqs.q: When should I be concerned about my child’s weight & growth patterns?
faqs.a: Consult a physician if your kid falls between the two main percentile lines, stops developing for several months, or gains weight that exceeds their height. Look for physical red flags such as severe exhaustion, unusual thirst, or a lack of sexual maturity by age 13 for females and 14 for boys. Finally, any major differences from recognized growth trends or inability to reach developmental goals require a professional evaluation.

faqs.q: What are the signs of Precocious Puberty in children?
faqs.a: Precocious puberty refers to the start of physical sexual development, such as breast growth or testicular enlargement, before the age of eight in girls and nine in boys. Aside from early hair development and acne, a quick growth spurt is common, which might ironically result in a lower total adult stature if the bones mature too soon. You should also look for emotional changes, such as increased irritability or moodiness, that accompany these premature physical changes.

faqs.q: When should I see a pediatric endocrinologist for my child’s growth issues?
faqs.a: Medical problems develop when a kid grows slower than 4-5 cm per year, falls drastically below their projected height, or has a dramatic decline in growth chart percentiles. Parents should keep an eye out for puberty before the age of eight in females and nine in boys, as well as a lack of development by the ages of thirteen and fourteen. Persistent weight swings, an enlarged thyroid, or excessive thirst and urination may all suggest hormonal or metabolic disorders.

faqs.q: How is Polycystic Ovary Syndrome diagnosed in adolescents?
faqs.a: Adolescent PCOS is diagnosed by combining chronic monthly irregularity at least one to three years after menarche with indications of elevated androgen levels, such as severe acne or hirsutism. Blood tests must be used to rule out endocrine abnormalities that mirror one another, as pelvic ultrasounds are frequently inconclusive owing to typical pubertal ovarian changes. If an adolescent fits only one of these requirements, they are often labeled as “at risk” and subjected to ongoing monitoring rather than obtaining a quick, conclusive diagnosis.

faqs.q: What treatments are available for Hypothyroidism in children?
faqs.a: The standard therapy for congenital or acquired hypothyroidism is levothyroxine, which must be provided at exact age- and weight-based doses and monitored with monthly blood tests. To promote proper absorption and minimize developmental delays, parents should avoid administering this medication together with soy formula, iron, or calcium supplements.

faqs.q: How is Type 1 Diabetes managed in children?
faqs.a: To effectively control juvenile type 1 diabetes, a mix of rapid-acting and long-acting insulin, frequently supplied through a pump, must be used in conjunction with continuous glucose monitoring. Families must learn carbohydrate counting to balance their diet with insulin dosages while maintaining certain protocols so that school staff can assist with blood sugar checks and emergency snacks. To avoid issues, youngsters should exercise every day and have quarterly check-ups.

faqs.q: What are the success rates for treating childhood obesity with endocrinology interventions?
faqs.a: The success rates for treating childhood obesity using endocrinology therapies, particularly newer anti-obesity drugs (AOMs), are generally encouraging, with many trials indicating a 5% to 18% drop in body mass index (BMI). While traditional lifestyle programs frequently produce moderate outcomes (about 5-10% effectiveness in lowering BMI z-scores after two years), pharmaceutical strategies have demonstrated greater efficacy in teenagers.

faqs.q: Can a vitamin deficiency cause foot pain?
faqs.a: u003cpu003eu003cspan style=u0022font-weight: 400;u0022u003eFoot pain is frequently caused by a lack of vitamin D, which produces agonizing bone pain, or a deficiency in B-complex vitamins (B1, B6, and B12), which causes nerve burning and tingling. Additionally, a shortage of magnesium and calcium can lead to muscular weakness and illnesses such as plantar fasciitis. To address these symptoms, see a doctor for blood tests before beginning any dietary modifications or supplements, such as high-dose B12 or multivitamins.u003c/spanu003eu003c/pu003e

faqs.q: Can your feet give an early warning about serious health problems?
faqs.a: u003cpu003eBecause your feet are the furthest from your heart, they frequently show the earliest indications of systemic disorders, including diabetes, heart disease, and peripheral artery disease, such as numbness, coldness, or slow-healing sores. Certain bodily changes, such as unexplained swelling, hair loss on the toes, or skin discoloration, may serve as early warning signs of severe problems, including renal failure, poor circulation, or thyroid abnormalities. Persistent foot soreness should not be overlooked, as a professional review of these u0022early warning signsu0022 can lead to a quick diagnosis and avert serious long-term problems.u003c/pu003e

faqs.q: Is podiatry covered by health insurance?
faqs.a: u003cpu003eAt Yashoda Hospitals, our expert physiotherapists, orthopedic surgeons, and reconstructive microsurgeons look forward to discussing your options and helping you make informed decisions. Additionally, healthcare insurance may partially cover the cost of artificial disc replacement surgery, so it’s essential to check with your insurance provider. You can also explore government subsidies or policies that may help you manage various ADR surgery costs at Yashoda Hospitals in India.u003c/pu003e

faqs.q: What foot problems do children need podiatry for?
faqs.a: u003cpu003ePediatric podiatry examines developmental foot disorders such as flat feet, gait deviations such as toe walking, and congenital deformities to enable correct biomechanical growth. Custom orthotics, exercises, and footwear advice are used by specialists to treat common juvenile disorders such as painful ingrown toenails, plantar warts, and sports-related injuries such as Sever’s disease. To avoid long-term mobility difficulties, parents should seek professional assistance if their child is experiencing persistent discomfort, frequent stumbling, or a noticeable change in their walking pattern.u003c/pu003e

faqs.q: How do I find a reputable podiatry clinic near me?
faqs.a: u003cpu003eYou may get excellent podiatrist services in Hyderabad by searching the official Yashoda Hospitals website for specialists in foot and ankle care. Look for board-certified experts with substantial knowledge in areas such as diabetic foot care, sports injuries, and advanced wound healing. These specialists utilize the latest technology and minimally invasive treatments in key locations such as Malakpet, Secunderabad, and Sambalpur to deliver excellent outcomes. Before booking, you can verify a specialist’s credentials and hospital affiliations directly through the hospital’s department listings to find the best fit for your specific needs.u003c/pu003e

faqs.q: How long does it take to recover from common foot treatments?
faqs.a: u003cpu003eFor mild sprains, it can take two to four weeks for the foot to recover. For significant fractures or reconstructive surgery, it can take several months. Sometimes, the swelling can linger up to four months. Rest, elevation, and the use of prescription orthopedic boots or physical therapy are all critical components of successful healing. Early professional intervention and advanced surgical procedures may significantly accelerate this process, allowing some patients to resume carrying weights much quicker than old approaches permitted.u003c/pu003e

faqs.q: How can I prevent recurring foot problems?
faqs.a: u003cpu003eTo keep your feet healthy, wear supportive, well-fitting footwear and practice everyday cleanliness, such as cleaning and drying thoroughly between your toes to prevent infection. To avoid ingrown toenails, cut them straight across and engage in low-impact activities or stretching to strengthen your feet while maintaining your body weight. It is critical to avoid going barefoot and to examine your feet regularly for any wounds, redness, or non-healing sores that may need expert treatment. Consulting a podiatrist is vital for safe and efficient treatment of recurrent concerns such as corns, calluses, or trouble with nail care, particularly for individuals with diabetes.u003c/pu003e

faqs.q: What are the symptoms of thalassemia in children?
faqs.a: Children with thalassemia often have pale skin, jaundice, a swollen spleen or liver, causing the stomach to swell, slow growth, changes in the bones in their faces, and feelings of tiredness or inability to handle exercise. Severe instances may involve black urine and developmental impairments. These symptoms result from decreased hemoglobin synthesis, which leads to anemia.

faqs.q: What are the signs that my child may need a blood transfusion?
faqs.a: A child may require a transfusion if his or her hemoglobin level falls below 9-10.5 g/dL before the transfusion or if they exhibit symptoms such as fatigue, breathing problems, an enlarged spleen, bone changes, or decreased growth despite higher levels. Transfusions keep levels stable to support development and prevent problems.

faqs.q: When should I be concerned about my child’s blood test results?
faqs.a: If hemoglobin is low (e.g., <9 g/dL in thalassemia), there is an inadequate reticulocyte response, symptoms of hemolysis, or rapid dips producing dizziness, a pale/yellow complexion, or breathing difficulties. Rapid decreases or poor bone marrow response require immediate evaluation.

faqs.q: What tests are used to diagnose blood disorders in children?
faqs.a: Common tests include complete blood count (CBC), hemoglobin electrophoresis, reticulocyte count, genetic testing, iron studies, and coagulation tests, including PT/PTT. Bone marrow tests or blood smears can confirm conditions such as thalassemia or clotting problems.

faqs.q: What treatments are available for childhood leukemia?
faqs.a: Chemotherapy is the primary treatment, typically combined with immunotherapy such as blinatumomab or CAR T-cell therapy for high-risk patients. Radiation, targeted medicines, and stem cell transplants are among the treatment options. Chemotherapy is commonly used as the initial line of treatment for most children.

faqs.q: How is hemophilia managed in children?
faqs.a: Children with hemophilia are treated with frequent preventative infusions of clotting factor concentrates (VIII for A and IX for B) to avoid bleeding, particularly early in life. Active bleeds are treated with on-demand medication, which also includes medicines for mucosal bleeding and for mild episodes. Lifestyle changes include safe activities, vaccines, and multidisciplinary care for joints and general health.

faqs.q: How is iron deficiency anemia treated in children?
faqs.a: Treatment consists of oral iron supplementation at 3 mg/kg/day of elemental iron for three months after hemoglobin has normalized, addressing factors such as diet or blood loss. Dietary changes with iron-rich foods (meats, beans, fortified cereals) paired with vitamin C enhance absorption. If oral iron fails, IV iron is employed; the response indicates a 1 g/dL increase in hemoglobin over a month.

faqs.q: What are the common conditions treated by pediatric nephrologists?
faqs.a: Pediatric nephrologists diagnose and treat common kidney and urinary tract disorders in children, including urinary tract infections, nephrotic syndrome, kidney stones, chronic kidney disease, hypertension, and congenital abnormalities.

faqs.q: What are the symptoms of kidney disorders in children?
faqs.a: Edema in the face, legs, or ankles; changes in the urine, such as blood, protein, or dark color; decreased urine production; exhaustion; stunted development; and elevated blood pressure are common symptoms in children with kidney diseases.

faqs.q: When should my child see a pediatric nephrologist immediately?
faqs.a: If your child exhibits any of the following symptoms: extreme swelling, very little or no urine, a persistent high temperature, blood in the urine, severe flank pain, disorientation, or seizures, you should seek medical attention right away, since these are signs of acute kidney damage or failure.

faqs.q: When should I take my child to see a pediatric nephrologist?
faqs.a: Consult a pediatric nephrologist if you have chronic urinary problems, recurring infections, abnormal urine tests, hypertension, or kidney-related developmental delays, which are commonly referred to by your doctor.

faqs.q: How can I prepare my child for a visit to the pediatric nephrologist?
faqs.a: Explain the appointment clearly, ensure that it is not intimidating, bring your medical history, growth records, urine/blood test results, and a urine sample if necessary, and take deep breaths to reduce nervousness.

faqs.q: What treatments are available for urinary tract infections in children?
faqs.a: Children’s urinary tract infections are treated with antibiotics customized to the illness type and dosed based on weight and age; hydration, pain medication, and follow-up tests help avoid recurrence.

faqs.q: How is nephrotic syndrome treated in children?
faqs.a: Nephrotic syndrome is controlled with corticosteroids to promote remission, diuretics for edema, ACE inhibitors for proteinuria, and dietary adjustments; severe cases may require immunosuppressants or dialysis.

faqs.q: What types of cancers are commonly treated in pediatric oncology?
faqs.a: Pediatric oncology often addresses leukemias (particularly acute lymphoblastic leukemia), lymphomas, brain and central nervous system malignancies, neuroblastoma, Wilms tumor (kidney cancer), and bone cancers such as osteosarcoma and Ewing sarcoma. Specialized teams manage these malignancies, which differ in biology and behavior from adult-type tumors.

faqs.q: How can childhood cancer be prevented?
faqs.a: The majority of childhood malignancies are still incurable because they are caused by hereditary or developmental factors rather than lifestyle decisions. Avoiding tobacco-related smoking, minimizing radiation exposure, keeping a balanced diet and physical activity, and timely vaccinations (e.g., HPV) may lower cancer-related risks.

faqs.q: What are the symptoms of childhood cancer?
faqs.a: Common red flag signs include a new lump or swelling, unexplained weight loss, intense weariness, chronic discomfort, frequent or unexplained fevers, easy bruising or bleeding, and recurring headaches usually followed by vomiting. Other warning symptoms include sudden eye or vision changes, limping or difficulty walking, and a milky-white appearance of the eye (particularly in babies).

faqs.q: When should I take my child to see a pediatric oncologist?
faqs.a: Consult a pediatric oncologist if your child’s symptoms continue or worsen (for example, unexplained tumors, extended fevers, extreme weight loss, or severe, unexplained pain) despite usual treatment. Pediatric clinics often respond to suspected cancer referrals within 24-48 hours based on laboratory or physical findings.

faqs.q: How is pediatric cancer diagnosed?
faqs.a: Diagnosis often begins with blood tests (complete blood count and chemistry panels), urine tests, and imaging (ultrasound, X-ray, CT, MRI, or PET/bone scan), followed by biopsy or cerebrospinal fluid sampling. A pathologist verifies the cancer kind, and molecular or genetic studies aid in narrowing the subtype and guiding treatment.

faqs.q: When should I seek a second opinion regarding my child’s cancer diagnosis?
faqs.a: A second opinion is recommended if the diagnosis or treatment plan is unclear, extremely complex, or if you are unsure about the risks and advantages of the planned therapy. Many pediatric cancer clinics accept second opinions, particularly for uncommon malignancies or newly discovered targeted medicines or clinical trials.

faqs.q: How is chemotherapy used in treating childhood cancer?
faqs.a: Chemotherapy is the most frequent treatment for juvenile malignancies because many pediatric tumors develop quickly and are responsive to cytotoxic medications. It is often administered in cycles (treatment days plus recuperation times) and can be supplemented with surgery, radiation, or newer targeted/immunotherapy drugs.

faqs.q: What are the symptoms of asthma in children?
faqs.a: Common symptoms include wheezing or whistling when exhaling, shortness of breath, fast breathing, coughing (particularly at night or after exercise), and chest discomfort. Infants may have problems eating or sucking. Repeated bronchitis or pneumonia episodes may also indicate asthma.

faqs.q: What are the symptoms of bronchitis in children?
faqs.a: Symptoms include a dry cough that turns into mucus, wheezing on exercise, shortness of breath with chest pain, a runny nose, and fever with muscular aches. Coughing usually lasts for weeks.

faqs.q: When should I take my child to see a pediatric pulmonologist?
faqs.a: Refer if your child has a recurrent cough or wheeze that lasts more than two days per week for weeks, persistent nasal congestion, exercise-induced cough, frequent asthma hospitalizations, or is refractory to routine treatment. Also used for moderate to severe chronic asthma, frequent oral steroids, or abnormal spirometry.

faqs.q: When should I seek emergency care for my child’s breathing difficulties?
faqs.a: Those struggling to breathe, with blue lips, sleepiness, difficulty speaking in complete phrases, rapid/shallow breathing, or severe wheezing/coughing that cannot be alleviated by medicine, or pale/blue skin should be taken to the emergency room or have an ambulance called. Seek medical attention if your kid is really unhappy, has rapid/noisy breathing, or requires a reliever more than once every three hours.

faqs.q: How is cystic fibrosis diagnosed in children?
faqs.a: Diagnosis is usually started with newborn screening, followed by a sweat chloride test (saltier sweat) on the skin after age 2 weeks. Genetic testing and other laboratories support it.

faqs.q: How is sleep apnea diagnosed and treated in children?
faqs.a: Polysomnography (PSG), or respiratory polygraphy, is used to diagnose the apnea-hypopnea index; screening includes a history, physical, and questions. Treatment steps include weight loss if obese, nasal steroids/montelukast, adenotonsillectomy for hypertrophy, CPAP if chronic, and orthodontics.

faqs.q: How is pneumonia treated in children?
faqs.a: Mild cases require rest, drinks, a humidifier, acetaminophen, and cough treatment. Severe: intravenous or oral antibiotics, oxygen, fluids, suctioning, and breathing therapies.

A pediatric transplant is a surgical operation that replaces a child’s damaged or failing organ with a healthy organ from a donor, typically indicating when a child’s organ has been significantly damaged by illness or congenital problems and is unable to function normally despite medical intervention. When alternative treatments fail to manage the condition, a transplant is recommended because it provides the highest chance of survival and a better quality of life.
What are the common conditions that may require a pediatric transplant?

Several significant medical disorders might necessitate the need for an organ transplant in youngsters. These include congenital heart abnormalities, chronic kidney failure, severe liver illnesses, including biliary atresia, hereditary metabolic disorders, advanced lung diseases like cystic fibrosis, and bone marrow disorders that impact blood cell synthesis. Some children may need a transplant as a result of organ damage caused by infections, autoimmune illnesses, or genetic abnormalities that affect normal organ function.

Pediatric transplants are not diagnosed in the same way as diseases are; instead, clinicians do a thorough medical review to decide whether a child requires a transplant. Experts evaluate the child’s overall health, the severity of the organ ailment, and the effectiveness of alternative therapies. The transplant team evaluates the child’s medical history, imaging data, laboratory findings, and past therapy responses. If the organ damage is irreversible and life-threatening, the child may be eligible for a transplant.

This ensures that every child gets the best care and infection prevention available.

faqs.q: What is a pediatric transplant, and when is it necessary?
faqs.a: A pediatric transplant is a surgical operation that replaces a child’s damaged or failing organ with a healthy organ from a donor, typically indicating when a child’s organ has been significantly damaged by illness or congenital problems and is unable to function normally despite medical intervention. When alternative treatments fail to manage the condition, a transplant is recommended because it provides the highest chance of survival and a better quality of life.

faqs.q: What are the common conditions that may require a pediatric transplant?
faqs.a: Several significant medical disorders might necessitate the need for an organ transplant in youngsters. These include congenital heart abnormalities, chronic kidney failure, severe liver illnesses, including biliary atresia, hereditary metabolic disorders, advanced lung diseases like cystic fibrosis, and bone marrow disorders that impact blood cell synthesis. Some children may need a transplant as a result of organ damage caused by infections, autoimmune illnesses, or genetic abnormalities that affect normal organ function.

faqs.q: What are the symptoms that indicate my child may need a transplant?
faqs.a: Symptoms vary depending on the organ involved, but may include chronic fatigue, poor development or weight gain, severe breathing issues, body swelling, frequent infections, jaundice (yellowing of the skin or eyes), decreased urine output, or declining heart or lung function. In many circumstances, children with severe organ failure may have developmental delays or difficulties doing everyday tasks. If the child’s symptoms worsen after treatment, doctors may consider a transplant.

faqs.q: How is a pediatric transplant diagnosed?
faqs.a: Pediatric transplants are not diagnosed in the same way as diseases are; instead, clinicians do a thorough medical review to decide whether a child requires a transplant. Experts evaluate the child’s overall health, the severity of the organ ailment, and the effectiveness of alternative therapies. The transplant team evaluates the child’s medical history, imaging data, laboratory findings, and past therapy responses. If the organ damage is irreversible and life-threatening, the child may be eligible for a transplant.

faqs.q: What treatments are available for children awaiting a transplant?
faqs.a: While waiting for a transplant, children may receive supportive therapy to maintain their health and keep their organs functioning as long as feasible. These therapies differ based on the damaged organ and may involve medications, nutritional therapy, oxygen support, renal dialysis, or specialist heart or lung support equipment. Regular monitoring and medical treatment ensure the child remains stable and healthy enough to undergo transplantation once a suitable donor organ becomes available.

faqs.q: What surgical procedures are involved in pediatric organ transplantation?
faqs.a: Pediatric organ transplantation entails removing the diseased organ and replacing it with a viable donor organ. The technique varies depending on the organ being transplanted, but it often involves connecting the donor organ’s blood arteries and ducts to the child’s body to restore normal function. The procedure is conducted by experienced transplant surgeons and necessitates modern operating rooms and post-operative critical care. After surgery, children are given immunosuppressants to prevent the immune system from rejecting the transplanted organ.

faqs.q: When should I consider seeing a pediatric transplant specialist?
faqs.a: If a child’s organ illness is severe or progressing and is resistant to routine therapy, parents should see a pediatric transplant expert. Early referral is critical when clinicians detect evidence of organ failure, increasing symptoms, or complications that could harm survival or quality of life. A transplant specialist can assess the child’s health, determine whether transplantation is suitable, and help families navigate the assessment, donor matching, and treatment procedure.

faqs.q: How is pediatric neurology different from adult neurology?
faqs.a: Pediatric neurologists treat infants, children, and adolescents, while adult neurologists focus on adults. Pediatric neurologists account for brain development, as it affects how disorders present. Causes and treatments for neurological issues often differ between children and adults. Pediatric neurologists undergo specialized training to manage conditions like cerebral palsy, brain injuries, and developmental delays, while both pediatric and adult neurologists treat headaches, seizures, and epilepsy.

faqs.q: What Is the difference between a paediatrician and a paediatric urologist?
faqs.a: A paediatrician provides general medical care for children, including routine checkups, vaccinations, and treatment for common illnesses and minor urinary issues. A paediatric urologist specialises in diagnosing and treating complex urinary and genital conditions in children, including congenital abnormalities, kidney disorders, and conditions requiring surgical intervention.

faqs.q: Do all urological diseases in children require surgery?
faqs.a: No, not all paediatric urological conditions require surgery. Many can be managed with medication, lifestyle changes, or non-invasive treatments. Surgery is recommended only when necessary for the child’s health and well-being.

faqs.q: Can urological surgeries in children be made less painful?
faqs.a: Yes, advancements like robotic-assisted surgery are minimally invasive techniques that help to reduce pain, scarring, and recovery time, making the surgical experience more comfortable for children. To know more about robotic-assisted pediatric urological surgery, explore our blog for detailed overview, expert insights and benefits on Robotic Assisted Pediatric Urology.

faqs.q: Can a child safely undergo surgery involving the urogenital organs?
faqs.a: Yes, with expert paediatric urologists and advanced surgical technology like robotic urological surgeries in children are performed safely, ensuring the best possible outcomes with minimal discomfort and quicker recovery.

faqs.q: Why is it performed?
faqs.a: To diagnose, biopsy, and treat a variety of symptoms and conditions of the upper digestive tract, including the stomach, duodenum, and esophagus, for symptoms like heartburn, chronic weight loss, or chronic nausea.

faqs.q: Why is it performed?
faqs.a: Colonoscopy is a valuable diagnostic tool in pediatrics because it provides a thorough picture of the colon’s interior lining, allowing doctors to explore chronic symptoms such as unexplained abdominal pain, diarrhea, and rectal bleeding. It is critical for identifying and treating gastrointestinal diseases, like inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), as well as locating and removing malignant polyps.

faqs.q: Why is it performed?
faqs.a: Bronchoscopy : It is a vital procedure for pediatric respiratory care, used to investigate persistent symptoms, like unexplained cough or wheezing, and is essential for diagnosing and safely removing foreign objects from the airways. It also plays a key role in identifying chronic lung conditions and collecting fluid or tissue samples through bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) for infection and inflammation analysis.

faqs.q: Why is it performed?
faqs.a: The G-tube supplies necessary calories and nutrients, making it an important intervention for growth and management of severe gastrointestinal disorders, as well as when other feeding systems, such as an NG-tube, are not tolerated. The G-tube supplies vital calories and nutrients, making it an important intervention for growth and controlling severe gastrointestinal disorders, or when other feeding systems, such as an NG-tube, are not tolerated.

faqs.q: Why is it performed?
faqs.a: A Central Line is placed in children to allow for the safe and long-term delivery of critical treatments, bypassing the need for frequent needle pokes into smaller veins. It is primarily used for the administration of chemotherapy for cancer, Intravenous (IV) Nutrition, and prolonged courses of antibiotics or IV fluids, while also facilitating easy and frequent collection of blood samples and transfusions.

faqs.q: Why is it performed?
faqs.a: Babies are hospitalized in the Neonatal Intensive Care Unit (NICU) for critical care for a variety of reasons, including premature birth (before 37 weeks), low birth weight, and severe respiratory issues, since their immature bodies require specialized care. The NICU also treats critical illnesses, such as infections (sepsis), birth abnormalities that impact organs, and metabolic disorders like hypoglycemia.

faqs.q: Why is it performed?
faqs.a: A Pediatric Intensive Care Unit (PICU) provides rapid, specialized care to children with life-threatening diseases or recuperating from major surgery. Severe infections (sepsis), traumatic injuries, organ failure, and serious respiratory distress requiring advanced mechanical assistance are some of the most common reasons for PICU admission.

faqs.q: Why is it performed?
faqs.a: NICU care is critical for newborns who require specialized medical attention owing to factors such as early delivery (before 37 weeks) or low birth weight (less than 2.5 kg), as their organs may be undeveloped. It provides intensive monitoring and specialist treatment to neonates with critical health issues, such as congenital defects or delivery complications.

pages.title: Best Physiotherapy Hospital in Hyderabad, India
overview: Physiotherapy, also known as Physical Therapy, is a healthcare profession focused on improving and maintaining the best possible physical function throughout a person’s life. It helps in returning the body to its normal function and prevents disabilities caused by illness, injury, or trauma.

Over the years, physiotherapy has earned its place as the trusted first line of care for a wide range of aches, stiffness, and posture-related discomforts. Be it a strained neck, a recurring backache, or muscular tightness, people instinctively turn to physiotherapy — not just for relief, but for long-term restoration. Its non-invasive approach and proven results have made it a household name in pain management and functional recovery.

The Physiotherapy specialists at Yashoda Hospitals have years of experience in providing the best physical therapy by adapting comprehensive examination and appropriate investigation to provide treatment and advice for individuals with movement or functional dysfunction, malfunction, disorder, disability, and pain from trauma and disease, using physical modalities including exercise, mobilization, manipulations, electrical and thermal agents and other electro therapeutics for prevention, health promotion and fitness, making us stand as one of the best physiotherapy hospitals in Hyderabad, India

As the top physiotherapy hospital in hyderabad, our department excels in providing physical therapy across different specialties such as heart and lung health (cardiopulmonary), care for older adults (geriatrics), nervous system disorders ( neurological ), sports injuries, hands-on therapy (manual), bone and muscle health ( orthopedic ), and children’s health ( pediatrics ).

Our team of therapists are graduates and postgraduates from reputed universities across India with specializations in orthopedics , sports, cardiopulmonary, neurology , geriatrics, women’s and child health , oncology , pediatrics , and critical care setting the world class standards in physiocare making us the top physiotherapy hospital in Hyderabad, India.

In pursuit of continuous learning, our physiotherapy specialists actively participate in nationally and internationally accredited workshops to stay updated with the latest advancements and technologies in the field.

faqs.q: What conditions can physiotherapy help with?
faqs.a: Physiotherapy can help manage a wide range of conditions including joint and muscle pain, post-surgical recovery, neurological disorders (like stroke and Parkinson’s), sports injuries, respiratory issues, and pediatric developmental delays.

faqs.q: Do I need a referral from a doctor to see a physiotherapist?
faqs.a: While a referral can be helpful, it is not always required. You can book a consultation directly with our physiotherapy department for an initial assessment.

faqs.q: What is the role of physiotherapy in stroke and paralysis patients?
faqs.a: Physiotherapy helps stroke and paralysis patients regain movement, strength, and independence. It improves balance, coordination, and mobility while reducing muscle stiffness and preventing complications

faqs.q: How many physiotherapy sessions will I need?
faqs.a: The number of sessions varies depending on the condition and its severity. Your physiotherapist will provide a treatment schedule after the initial assessment.

faqs.q: Is physiotherapy painful?
faqs.a: Physiotherapy is generally not painful. Some discomfort may occur during exercises or treatments, but your therapist will ensure it stays within a tolerable range and adjust the approach as needed.

pages.title: Best Psychiatric Hospital in Hyderabad
overview: 1 in every 8 people in the world live with a mental disorder (WHO). A mental disorder is characterized by a clinically significant disturbance in an individual’s cognition, emotional regulation, or behaviour. It is usually associated with distress or impairment in important areas of functioning. There are many different types of mental disorders. Mental disorders may also be referred to as mental health conditions. The latter is a broader term covering mental disorders, psychosocial disabilities and (other) mental states associated with significant distress, impairment in functioning, or risk of self-harm.

faqs.q: Why Choose Yashoda Hospitals?
faqs.a: Experienced board-certified psychiatrists

faqs.q: What are the psychiatric care facilities available at Yashoda Hospitals?
faqs.a: Yashoda Hospitals offer specialized psychiatric care for individuals of all age groups. Services include treatment for anxiety, depression, bipolar disorder, and schizophrenia in adults; emotional and behavioral support for children and adolescents; and focused care for mental health issues in the elderly.

faqs.q: What is the treatment for Schizophrenia?
faqs.a: Pharmacological treatment is the first treatment of choice. Long acting injectables may be preferred in patients who have poor drug compliance to oral medication. Schizophrenia is a chronic type of psychiatric disorder which needs long term medication and regular monitoring of the course of medication and response

faqs.q: Is there a risk of developing dependency on medications prescribed for mental health conditions?
faqs.a: Medications prescribed for psychological disorders, such as antidepressants, antipsychotics, mood stabilizers, and anti-anxiety drugs, are designed to help regulate brain chemistry and manage symptoms effectively. Most of these medications do not cause dependency in the way substances like alcohol or narcotics do.

faqs.q: Is it mandatory to take psychiatric medication throughout life?
faqs.a: The duration of psychiatric medication depends on the type and severity of the mental health condition, individual response to treatment. The course of medication and response to the medication should be regularly reviewed by a psychiatrist. Stopping medication without professional guidance can lead to relapse and worsen the symptoms.

faqs.q: Can psychiatric conditions be genetically inherited?
faqs.a: Certain psychiatric conditions can have a genetic component. Research shows that disorders such as depression, bipolar disorder, schizophrenia, and anxiety may run in families, suggesting a hereditary link.

faqs.q: Why do patients in ICU usually suffer from a brief period of psychosis?
faqs.a: Patients in the ICU often experience a brief period of psychosis, commonly referred to as ICU delirium. This is usually caused by a combination of factors such as severe illness, infections, low oxygen levels, and the side effects of medications like sedatives or painkillers.

pages.title: Best Lung Hospital in Hyderabad
overview: The paramount importance of healthy lungs cannot be overstated, as their direct impact affects the entire body’s functioning. The devastating effects of compromised lung health, including severe outcomes like death, have placed immense responsibility on pulmonologists, especially during the COVID-19 pandemic.

Yashoda Hospitals’ Department of Pulmonary and Lung Sciences boasts a team of the best pulmonologists in Hyderabad with highly experienced critical care intensivists and senior pulmonologists who have been playing a crucial role, offering expert care round the clock and saving thousands of lives during the COVID-19 pandemic, making it one of the best pulmonology hospitals in Hyderabad, India.

The alarming increase in pollution and the surge of toxic irritants in the air have created a critical health crisis in recent times. We are now more vulnerable than ever to early-onset breathing difficulties and chronic lung conditions. With every breath, we are exposed to harmful pollutants that penetrate deep into our lungs, leading to a rise in respiratory diseases, including asthma , bronchitis, and even lung cancer . In these challenging times, the expertise of pulmonologists and lung care specialists has become more crucial than ever.

It’s increasingly common for households to have children or family members affected by respiratory conditions such as asthma, persistent cough, allergies , or airway diseases like COPD , bronchiolitis, and bronchiectasis, often triggered by smoking, pollution, or exposure to irritants. The treatment plan is unique and specifically constructed, tailored to every patient’s medical history and physical condition. We offer care for children with pulmonary conditions that respect the family’s wishes, emphasizing effective treatment and compassionate support. The hospital has a dedicated asthma unit exclusively for treating the most severe cases of asthma with minimally invasive techniques like Bronchial Thermoplasty , which has been performed successfully in the twin states.

Our multidisciplinary approach, working closely with intensivists, enables us to effectively manage the most complex lung diseases and infections. The hospital sees a large volume of patients suffering from conditions like pneumonia (bacterial, viral, fungal, and TB), influenza, COVID-19, or interstitial and inflammatory diseases like sarcoidosis, ILD, hypersensitivity pneumonia, connective tissue diseases (CTD), vasculitis, pleural effusion, pneumothorax, and empyema daily.
Surgery for lung & esophageal cancer & other thoracic tumors, including complex resections
